analogy: Think of "US Hegemony" through the chapter's own opening images: O verview Let us shift our gaze from the larger global developments in the post- Cold War era to developments in our own region, South Asia. When India and Pakistan joined the club of nuclear powers, this region suddenly became the focus of global attention.... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
US Hegemony is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that o verview Let us shift our gaze from the larger global developments in the post- Cold War era to developments in our own region, South Asia.

The text explains that when India and Pakistan joined the club of nuclear powers, this region suddenly became the focus of global attention.

The text explains that the focus was, of course, on the various kinds of conflict in this region: there are pending border and water sharing disputes between the states of the region.

The text explains that besides, there are conflicts arising out of insurgency, ethnic strife and resource sharing.

The text explains that at the same time, many people in South Asia recognise the fact that this region can develop and prosper if the states of the region cooperate with each other.

The text explains that in this chapter, we try to understand the nature of conflict and cooperation among different countries of the region.

The text explains that since much of this is rooted in or conditioned by the domestic politics of these countries, we first introduce the region and the domestic politics of some of the big countries in the region.

The text explains that chapter 3 Contemporary South Asia Source: Subhas Rai’s adaptation of ‘Liberty Leading the People’, painted by Eugene Delacroix in 1830.

The text explains that courtesy of Himal Southasian, (January 2007) The Southasia Trust, Nepal

The text explains that contemporary World Politics 30 What is south asia?

The text explains that we are all familiar with the gripping tension during an India-Pakistan cricket match.

The text explains that we have also seen the goodwill and hospitality shown to visiting Indian and Pakistani fans by their hosts when they come to watch a cricket match.

The text explains that this is symbolic of the larger pattern of South Asian affairs.

The text explains that ours is a region where rivalry and goodwill, hope and despair, mutual suspicion and trust coexist.

The text explains that let us begin by asking an elementary question: what is South Asia?

The text explains that the expression ‘South Asia’ usually includes the following countries: Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, the Maldives, Nepal, Pakistan and Sri Lanka.

The text explains that source: SDSA Team, State of Democracy in South Asia, New Delhi: Oxford University Press, 2007 Countries SDG 3 Life expectancy at birth (years) 2017 SDG 4.6 Adult literacy rate (% ages 15 and older) 2006-2016 SDG 4.1 Gross enrolment ratio (Secondary) 2012-2017 SDG 8.1 GDP per capita (2011 PPP $) 2017 SDG 3.2 Infant mortality rate (per 1,000 live births) 2016 SDG 3.3 TB cases (per 100,000 people) 2016 SDG 1.1 Population living below income poverty line (%) PPP $1.90 a day 2006-2016 HDI Rank World 72.2 82.1 79 15,439 29.9 140.0 – – Developing 70.7 81.1 75 10,199 32.7 164.5 – – countries South Asia 69.3 68.7 71 6,485 37.8 206.3 – – Bangladesh 72.8 72.8 69 3,524 28.2 221.0 14.8 136 India 68.8 69.3 75 6,427 34.6 211.0 21.2 130 Nepal 70.6 59.6 71 2,433 28.4 154.0 15.0 149 Pakistan 66.6 57.0 46 5,035 64.2 268.0 6.1 150 Sri Lanka 75.5 91.2 98 11,669 8.0 65.0 – 76 Source: United Nations Development Programme, Human Development Report, 2018

The text explains that the Sri Lankan problem involves people of Indian origin, and there was considerable pressure from the Tamil people in India to the effect that the Indian government should protect the interests of the Tamils in Sri Lanka.

The text explains that but in 1987, the government of India for the first time got directly involved in the Sri Lankan Tamil question.

The text explains that since then India and Pakistan seem to have built a military relationship in which the possibility of a direct and full- scale war has declined.

The text explains that the dispute seems minor, but there is an underlying worry that how the dispute is settled may have an impact on the control of sea resources in the area adjoining Sir Creek.

The text explains that despite dif ferences, trade, scientific cooperation, common natural resources, electricity generation a n d i n t e r l o c k i n g w a t e r management grids hold the two countries together.

The text explains that india is involved in big hydroelectric projects in Bhutan and remains the Himalayan kingdom’s biggest source of development aid.

The text explains that what are some of the commonalities and differences between Bangladesh and Pakistan in their democratic experiences?

The text explains that name the principal players in the ethnic conflict in Sri Lanka.

The text explains that how do you assess the prospects of the resolution of this conflict?

The text explains that mention some of the recent agreements between India and Pakistan.

The text explains that can we be sure that the two countries are well on their way to a friendly relationship?

The text explains that mention two areas each of cooperation and disagreement between India and Bangladesh.

The text explains that how are the external powers influencing bilateral relations in South Asia?

The text explains that take any one example to illustrate your point.

The text explains that write a short note on the role and the limitations of SAARC as a forum for facilitating economic cooperation among the South Asian countries.

The text explains that india’s neighbours often think that the Indian government tries to dominate and interfere in the domestic affairs of the smaller countries of the region.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics, Chapter 3: US Hegemony

prelims_traps: Do not treat 31 Contemporary South Asia and TIMELINE OF SOUTH ASIA as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to US Hegemony; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain US Hegemony through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 31 Contemporary South Asia and TIMELINE OF SOUTH ASIA to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
